name: ujfcorp/doctrack
description: Automated Document Classification and Tracking System for Company Records Management Using Machine Learning (Laravel).
_comment_svm: php-ai/php-ml provides the SVM (SVC) + TF-IDF classifier used by App\Services\ClassificationService, matching the thesis. It uses php-ml's bundled libsvm (no PECL svm extension needed).
_comment_extraction_packages: smalot/pdfparser (pure PHP, always works) and thiagoalessio/tesseract_ocr (PHP wrapper) power TextExtractionService's hybrid pipeline (Scope 1.4). The Tesseract wrapper shells out to a system 'tesseract-ocr' binary for OCR of images/scanned PDFs — either a real system install (apt-get install tesseract-ocr) or, if that's not available (e.g. no root), the self-contained fallback copy already committed at storage/tesseract-bin, which TextExtractionService detects and uses automatically (see README section 2.3). TextExtractionService degrades gracefully (falls through to a validation_errors message) only if neither is present.
_comment_platform_pin: config.platform.php is pinned to 8.2.0 (matching the 'require.php' floor above) so composer update always resolves dependencies as if running on PHP 8.2, regardless of which PHP a contributor's own machine actually has installed. Without this, running composer update on a newer local PHP (e.g. 8.4) silently locks in transitive packages that require that newer PHP too, which then fails to install on any PHP 8.2/8.3 host (like Railway's build, or a grading panelist's machine) even though composer.json still claims ^8.2 support.
_comment_extensions: ext-zip and ext-fileinfo are declared explicitly (not just assumed present) for the same reason ext-pcntl is: a cloud build environment (Railway's Railpack) only installs PHP extensions it's told about, and both of these ship enabled by default on a typical local PHP install, which silently hid the gap during local development. ext-zip is required by TextExtractionService::extractFromDocx() (reads .docx via PHP's built-in ZipArchive) — without it, every .docx upload silently falls through to the OCR fallback instead of extracting real text. ext-fileinfo is required by App\Rules\ReliableMimeType (calls UploadedFile::getMimeType(), which needs fileinfo/finfo to sniff content) — without it, MIME verification for every non-image upload (pdf, txt) can behave unpredictably depending on Symfony's fallback guesser, while image types (png/jpg) still tend to succeed since their magic-byte signatures are recognized by more fallback paths.
